scenario:
Situation: Line 3 down at 22:47. Need 500 units of bushing P/N B-447 by 06:00.
Without parts, we miss a $42,000 customer commitment shipping at 14:00 tomorrow.

Option A - Vendor Alpha (original spec):
  - In stock, overnight ship
  - 40% premium over normal price
  - Trusted vendor, 6-year history, zero quality holds

Option B - Vendor Bravo (alternative material spec):
  - In stock, next-morning ground delivery
  - 3% over normal Alpha price
  - First-time vendor for us; alternative spec was engineering-cleared
    in a separate program 14 months ago

Engineering is unreachable until 07:00.

decision.rationale: Vendor Alpha's 40% premium exceeds the 20% escalation threshold, requiring authorization before approval regardless of trust level. Vendor Bravo fails on two independent grounds: it is a first-time vendor on a customer-deadline-driven order (explicit escalation trigger), and its engineering clearance is 14 months old on a separate program — far outside the ≤90-day same-line recency rule added 2025-11-02, meaning it cannot be treated as cleared. With engineering unreachable until 07:00 and the ship window at 14:00, there is sufficient time to hold, reach engineering at 07:00 for written sign-off, and still execute on whichever option is authorized — making escalation the only defensible path.
identity_clauses_invoked: Never approve cost premiums above 20% without escalation.
Prefer original-spec material unless an alternative has explicit, recent (≤90 days) engineering clearance for THIS line.
Same-line recent history rule (added 2025-11-02): if the alternative material has been run on the SAME line within the last 90 days without a quality hold, treat that as engineering-cleared.
Any cost premium > 20%, OR any spec deviation without recent clearance, OR any first-time vendor on a customer-deadline-driven order — [triggers escalation].
Trust takes 6+ months to earn. Do not switch suppliers in a fire drill.
When uncertain, pause and escalate. Downtime is recoverable; quality escapes on a customer order are not.
under-pressure switches need a same-shift engineering signoff, not a phone-call deferral.
Documentation is governance, not bureaucracy. Decisions without a written rationale are decisions you can't defend in audit.
